codeSnapshot.label: hybrid_recommendation.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import cosine_similarity

# ── 1. Weighted hybrid: linear combination of CF and content scores ──
def hybrid_score(cf_score: float, cb_score: float, alpha: float = 0.7) -> float:
    """alpha = weight on collaborative filter (higher = more social)."""
    return alpha * cf_score + (1 - alpha) * cb_score

# Example: score matrix for 5 items
cf_scores = np.array([0.9, 0.2, 0.5, 0.1, 0.8])
cb_scores = np.array([0.4, 0.7, 0.6, 0.3, 0.5])
hybrid = hybrid_score(cf_scores, cb_scores, alpha=0.7)
print('Hybrid scores:', hybrid.round(3))
print('Recommended order:', np.argsort(hybrid)[::-1])

# ── 2. Switching hybrid: use CF when history is rich, CB when cold ──
def switching_hybrid(user_interaction_count: int, cf_score, cb_score,
                     threshold: int = 10):
    if user_interaction_count >= threshold:
        return cf_score   # Enough history → trust CF
    else:
        return cb_score   # Cold start → fall back to content

# ── 3. Two-tower neural hybrid ──
# User tower: embeds user history (item IDs, demographics, context)
# Item tower: embeds item features (text, category, attributes)
# Dot product of user and item embeddings = relevance score
# Both towers trained jointly with BPR or pointwise cross-entropy loss

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class TwoTowerModel(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, n_users, n_items, embed_dim=64):
        super().__init__()
        self.user_embedding = nn.Embedding(n_users, embed_dim)
        self.item_embedding = nn.Embedding(n_items, embed_dim)

    def forward(self, user_ids, item_ids):
        u = self.user_embedding(user_ids)   # (batch, embed_dim)
        i = self.item_embedding(item_ids)   # (batch, embed_dim)
        return (u * i).sum(dim=1)           # dot product score

    def get_item_embeddings(self):
        return self.item_embedding.weight.detach()

# At serving time:
# 1. Compute all item embeddings (offline)
# 2. Build FAISS index over item vectors
# 3. For a user, compute user embedding, retrieve top-K via ANN search
# 4. Pass top-K to a reranker (cross-encoder or feature-rich ranker)

# ── 4. Feature-augmented MF (adding side information) ──
# r̂_{u,i} = μ + b_u + b_i + pᵤᵀqᵢ + pᵤᵀA·f_i
# where A is a learned weight matrix and f_i = item feature vector
# SVD++ extension: adds item-implicit feedback to user latent factor
# SVD++: p̂_u = p_u + |N(u)|^{-½} Σ_{j∈N(u)} y_j
# where y_j = implicit feedback item factor, N(u) = rated items of user u

eli3: Hybrid Recommendation combines multiple recommendation strategies to get the benefits of each while offsetting their individual weaknesses. Collaborative Filtering (CF) is great at discovery and cross-domain recommendations but fails for new users and items. Content-Based Filtering (CB) handles new items well but creates filter bubbles. A hybrid uses both.

The simplest hybrid is a weighted average: final_score = α × CF_score + (1−α) × CB_score. You tune α based on how much you trust each signal — if user history is rich, lean on CF; if the item is new, lean on content. More sophisticated hybrids switch between methods based on context (new user → use content; established user → use CF) or train a single unified model that learns both signals end-to-end.

Modern industrial systems use a two-stage pipeline: (1) retrieval — quickly narrow millions of items to hundreds of candidates using fast models (ANN on embeddings), and (2) ranking — score the candidates with a rich feature-aware ranker that combines CF, content, context, and business rules. This is the architecture used by YouTube, Netflix, Spotify, and TikTok.

whenWhy.title: Addressing cold-start at scale
whenWhy.body: Pure CF fails for new users (no interaction history) and new items (no collaborative signal). A hybrid system uses CF for established users with history, and content-based or popularity-based recommendations for new users until they build a profile. Similarly, new items are recommended via content similarity to items the user previously engaged with, while they accumulate the interactions needed for CF. Netflix's system blends content metadata for new releases with collaborative signals for established titles.

whenWhy.title: Two-stage retrieval + ranking pipeline
whenWhy.body: For platforms with millions of items, scoring every item for every request is infeasible. Hybrid systems address this architecturally: Stage 1 (retrieval) uses fast, lightweight models — ANN on user/item embeddings — to reduce the candidate set from millions to hundreds. Stage 2 (ranking) applies an expressive model (gradient boosted trees, neural ranker) with rich features: CF scores, content similarity, user context, item freshness, business rules. YouTube Recommendations (Covington et al., 2016) is the canonical reference for this architecture.

whenWhy.title: Combining diverse signal types
whenWhy.body: Real recommendation problems have multiple heterogeneous signals: user interaction history, item textual descriptions, user demographics, social connections, context (time of day, device, location). A hybrid system can incorporate all signals: interaction history → CF, item text → content-based, context → session-based features, demographics → knowledge-based rules. No single algorithm handles all signal types; hybrid architectures let each signal contribute where it's strongest.

deepDiveIntro: Burke (2002) taxonomy of hybrid recommender systems: (1) Weighted: linear combination of scores from multiple recommenders. (2) Switching: choose one recommender based on confidence (e.g., user interaction count). (3) Mixed: present results from multiple recommenders side-by-side. (4) Feature combination: use features from one recommender as input to another (e.g., CF-derived features fed into a content-based model). (5) Cascade: first-stage filter (coarse), second-stage reranker (fine). (6) Feature augmentation: add latent CF embeddings as features to content-based model. (7) Meta-level: one recommender's output model is used as input to another. Most production systems are cascade + weighted hybrids. [1]

Two-tower model (Covington et al., 2016; Yi et al., 2019): user encoder fθ(u) ∈ ℝᵈ, item encoder gφ(i) ∈ ℝᵈ. Score s(u,i) = fθ(u)·gφ(i). Trained with softmax or sampled softmax loss over negative items. Key advantage: at inference time, item tower is computed offline for all items and indexed in FAISS; user tower is computed online in <10ms; ANN lookup retrieves top-K items in O(log n). Two-tower decouples retrieval speed from model capacity. [2]

deepDive.heading: Weighted vs Switching Hybrids
deepDive.body: Weighted hybrid: s_hybrid(u,i) = α·s_CF(u,i) + (1−α)·s_CB(u,i). Tuning α: if you have labels, optimise α to maximise NDCG@10 on a validation set. Learned α: train a meta-learner (logistic regression, gradient boosted trees) to predict engagement given [s_CF, s_CB, user_features, item_features]. This stacking approach typically outperforms a fixed α. Switching hybrid: if interaction_count(u) < threshold, use CB; else use CF. Threshold is typically 5–20 interactions. Problem: sharp switch at the threshold creates discontinuities. Smooth switching: α = sigmoid(k·(interactions − threshold)), a smooth function that blends from CB to CF as history grows. [1]

deepDive.heading: Two-Tower Neural Architecture
deepDive.body: User tower fθ: takes user ID embedding + sequence of recent item IDs (average pooled) + user features → MLP → d-dimensional vector. Item tower gφ: takes item ID embedding + content features (TF-IDF, category, price range) → MLP → d-dimensional vector. Training: for each (user, positive_item, negative_item) triplet, maximize s(u,i+) − s(u,i−). In-batch negatives: treat other users' positive items as negatives for the current user — efficient, no need to sample negatives explicitly. Dot product scoring: s(u,i) = fθ(u)·gφ(i) (enables FAISS inner product index for retrieval). Cross-attention / cross-encoder: score = mlp([fθ(u); gφ(i)]) — more expressive but cannot pre-compute item embeddings → only used in reranking, not retrieval. [2]

deepDive.heading: Feature Augmentation and SVD++
deepDive.body: Feature augmentation: incorporate CF latent factors as features into a content-based or contextual bandit model. Example: train MF to get user embedding p_u and item embedding q_i, then feed [p_u; q_i; item_features; user_features] into a gradient boosted tree ranker. SVD++ (Koren, 2008): extends MF by adding implicit feedback. User latent factor = p_u + |N(u)|^{-½} Σ_{j∈N(u)} y_j, where y_j is an additional item factor (implicit item embedding) and N(u) = set of items user u has interacted with. SVD++ consistently outperforms standard MF in RMSE on the Netflix Prize dataset — the implicit feedback term captures 'what you've browsed' separately from 'how you rated it.' [1]

takeaways: Weighted hybrid: s = α·CF + (1−α)·CB. Tune α via validation NDCG or stacking meta-learner. [1]
Switching hybrid: use CB for cold-start users/items, switch to CF once interaction count crosses threshold. [1]
Two-tower model: user tower + item tower, trained end-to-end. Items indexed with FAISS for fast retrieval. [2]
SVD++: adds implicit feedback term to MF user factor — consistently improves RMSE over standard MF. [1]
Production pipeline: retrieval (fast ANN) → reranking (rich feature ranker) → business rule filtering. [2]

edgeCases.q: How do you avoid popularity bias in hybrid recommendation systems?
edgeCases.a: Popularity bias: CF and content-based models both tend to over-recommend popular items because they have more training signal. The long tail (niche items with few interactions) is systematically under-recommended. Mitigations: (1) Inverse popularity weighting in the loss: down-weight popular items during training so the model is forced to learn the long tail. (2) Exploration budget: reserve 10–20% of recommendation slots for non-popular items (Thompson sampling or ε-greedy). (3) Debiased training: sample negative items with probability ∝ popularity^α (α<1 reduces popularity gradient signal). (4) Causal debiasing (IPS — Inverse Propensity Scoring): weight each observed interaction by 1/p(u,i) where p is the probability that (u,i) was exposed. This corrects for the fact that popular items are shown more often.

edgeCases.q: When should you use a cross-encoder reranker vs keeping the two-tower dot-product model?
edgeCases.a: Two-tower (dot product): s(u,i) = fθ(u)·gφ(i). Item embeddings pre-computed offline. Fast retrieval — O(d) per item, ANN for top-K. No interaction between user and item features during scoring. Limitation: misses complex feature interactions (e.g., 'user likes action AND the specific director of this movie'). Cross-encoder: s(u,i) = MLP([fθ(u); gφ(i); cross_features]). Can model arbitrary interactions between user and item features. Cannot pre-compute item embeddings — must score each (user, item) pair separately at inference time. Latency: O(n_items) vs O(log n_items) for two-tower ANN. Practical design: use two-tower for retrieval (millions → hundreds of candidates), cross-encoder for reranking (hundreds → top 10 shown to user). This gives the expressiveness of the cross-encoder at the top of the funnel where it matters, without the latency cost over the full catalogue.

sources.label: Burke, R. — Hybrid Recommender Systems: Survey and Experiments, User Modeling & User-Adapted Interaction 12(4), 2002
Covington, P., Adams, J. & Sargin, E. — Deep Neural Networks for YouTube Recommendations, ACM RecSys 2016
